title: How To Trade Correlated Forex Pairs Without Losing Your A$$

transcript_segments.text: This is a question that used to haunt me
towards the beginning of my trading
journey. And it was, "What do you do
when two similar pairs have identical
setups?" So, for example, I'm going to
give you an example right here in which
CAD JPY and ADJPY, they both got similar
setups, right? They're both there at the
support zones here on the daily and
weekly charts. And I do think that price
is in fact going to maybe attempt to
bounce to the upside. So, they're two
perfectly viable trades, right? I could
take a buy right here at the support
zone on odd JPY with a stop below and a
target above or I can do a buy on KA JPY
with a stop below and a target above.
Now, I'm not one to predict because no
one can in fact predict what's going to
happen. But what I do know is that if
price is right here at the zone, it
gives me a good risk structure. But if I
were to take both trades, it would then
be doubling my risk and that's exposing
me to currency correlation because if
KJPY starts going down and breaking the
zone and goes to the stop-loss, ADJPY is
likely to do the same thing. So here's
the way I approach it. The simple way I
do this is I just pick what I believe is
the best trading opportunity. Now I know
this is subjective and it requires
experience and intuition and price
action discretion and context and things
such as that. I'm not one to hide that.
However, what I do believe is that when
you look at the charts, you can get a
little bit more information to help you
pick what the best setup is in fact is.
So whenever I look at odd JPY prices
come back into this zone and sure it's a
nice little zone. Yes. However, when we
go to KA JPY, it does look like we have
more interactions here at this zone that
make it just a little bit more clear
than ADJPY. So, for example, prices
bounced at resistance twice. It broke
above it bounced as support and then
again as support recently um about 2
weeks ago and now we're back here at
support again. I think CAD JPY is nicer
just because the move we had off this
zone was significantly longer outlived
for about 300 pips. Whereas on odd JPY
when price came here into this zone,
yes, we had a bounce but relatively it
just wasn't as far. It was 263 pips. it
did go in and make a new high. However,
I just do do think that KJPY is more
likely to snap back to the upside given
the variety of the market structure here
and the fact that it's a little bit
wider of a range. We've got more
potential for upside. So, what I've done
here is in the next trades group where I
call my personal trades, I've just
called a trade on KJPY. And so, what
I'll do right now before explaining this
is I'll actually go and just put this in
the free Telegram channel for you guys.
I'm just going to hit the screenshot to
copy the link to the chat image and then
I'll go here to Telegram and I'll post
it right here. So, CAD JPY is ready
for buys.
I have just entered
a buy and called the exact trade in the
MFX NT group, which is my Mission FX NIX
Trades Discord group. So, right here.
So, you can see right now what I've done
is I've risked $25,000 right there. And
then to go for a potential profit of
25,000. So basically what I'll do is
when price gets to the zone, I just go
to my MetaTrader 5 terminal on my mobile
phone. I have an iPhone, so I just go to
the MetaTrader 5 application. Then what
I do is enter a buy there at the zone. I
put a stop loss below the zone. And then
what I'll do is I'll increase the lot
size until that stop loss is reflective
of whatever my risk tolerance is.
Sometimes it's 25,000, sometimes it's
50K, sometimes it's 75K. It just depends
on the quality of the trading
opportunity. In this case, I felt
comfortable risking 25K, not anything
higher. So I've risked 25K on this.
After I do that and get the risk right,
then I just drag the stop loss, the
takeprofit to a 1:1 risk-reward ratio.
So in this case here, what we have is
we've got a trade setup and we've got a
trading opportunity that we've selected
based on what we thought was the better
idea. Now if you think that 80 JPY is a
better idea, or if you think GBP JPY is
better, which it in fact has broken the
zone from yesterday, then you may take
any of those that you please, but a good
rule of thumb is that you shouldn't be
in any more than one to two correlated
assets at once. If I felt comfortable
risking 50k on K JPY and a JPY and I
still want to elect to take both, which
is perfectly fine, then what I recommend
is that you just lower your position
sizing. So, for example, instead of
risking 50K on each, you're now exposed
to $100,000 on basically the same trade,
which is 80 JPY and K JPY. If they both
go up, you profit. If they both go down,
you lose. So, you're likely to lose both
trades or win both trades, depending on
what happens. You would lose 100K in
that situation. And it's basically like
doubling your risk on the same trade. So
what I do is I break it up if I want to
take both trading opportunities and I
say you know what instead of risking 50k
on K JPY and 50K on odd JPY I'll just
split up the risk. So I have one unit of
risk which is 50K on the same basic
trading opportunity and I'll risk 25K on
K JPY and then I'll risk 25K on odd JPY.
You can break up that risk because that
still accounts for the currency exposure
because these are conversely correlated
currencies. Anything with an XXX JPY in
the second quote pair, they're all going
to be roughly correlated. It's not
always perfect. Sure. But it does um
account for a lot of volatility. Way
better than just ignoring currency
correlation altogether. What I do
generally though is I just pick one
usually. Sometimes I'll go into two, but
not always. It's usually just I pick
whatever I think is the best one. So in
this case, price if it breaks below this
zone, I'm going to lose the trade and if
it pops up a little bit higher, then

full_text: This is a question that used to haunt me towards the beginning of my trading journey. And it was, "What do you do when two similar pairs have identical setups?" So, for example, I'm going to give you an example right here in which CAD JPY and ADJPY, they both got similar setups, right? They're both there at the support zones here on the daily and weekly charts. And I do think that price is in fact going to maybe attempt to bounce to the upside. So, they're two perfectly viable trades, right? I could take a buy right here at the support zone on odd JPY with a stop below and a target above or I can do a buy on KA JPY with a stop below and a target above. Now, I'm not one to predict because no one can in fact predict what's going to happen. But what I do know is that if price is right here at the zone, it gives me a good risk structure. But if I were to take both trades, it would then be doubling my risk and that's exposing me to currency correlation because if KJPY starts going down and breaking the zone and goes to the stop-loss, ADJPY is likely to do the same thing. So here's the way I approach it. The simple way I do this is I just pick what I believe is the best trading opportunity. Now I know this is subjective and it requires experience and intuition and price action discretion and context and things such as that. I'm not one to hide that. However, what I do believe is that when you look at the charts, you can get a little bit more information to help you pick what the best setup is in fact is. So whenever I look at odd JPY prices come back into this zone and sure it's a nice little zone. Yes. However, when we go to KA JPY, it does look like we have more interactions here at this zone that make it just a little bit more clear than ADJPY. So, for example, prices bounced at resistance twice. It broke above it bounced as support and then again as support recently um about 2 weeks ago and now we're back here at support again. I think CAD JPY is nicer just because the move we had off this zone was significantly longer outlived for about 300 pips. Whereas on odd JPY when price came here into this zone, yes, we had a bounce but relatively it just wasn't as far. It was 263 pips. it did go in and make a new high. However, I just do do think that KJPY is more likely to snap back to the upside given the variety of the market structure here and the fact that it's a little bit wider of a range. We've got more potential for upside. So, what I've done here is in the next trades group where I call my personal trades, I've just called a trade on KJPY. Then what I do is enter a buy there at the zone. I put a stop loss below the zone. And then what I'll do is I'll increase the lot size until that stop loss is reflective of whatever my risk tolerance is. Sometimes it's 25,000, sometimes it's 50K, sometimes it's 75K. It just depends on the quality of the trading opportunity. In this case, I felt comfortable risking 25K, not anything higher. So I've risked 25K on this. Now if you think that 80 JPY is a better idea, or if you think GBP JPY is better, which it in fact has broken the zone from yesterday, then you may take any of those that you please, but a good rule of thumb is that you shouldn't be in any more than one to two correlated assets at once. If I felt comfortable risking 50k on K JPY and a JPY and I still want to elect to take both, which is perfectly fine, then what I recommend is that you just lower your position sizing. So, for example, instead of risking 50K on each, you're now exposed to $100,000 on basically the same trade, which is 80 JPY and K JPY. If they both go up, you profit. If they both go down, you lose. So, you're likely to lose both trades or win both trades, depending on what happens. You would lose 100K in that situation. And it's basically like doubling your risk on the same trade.
